purpose: External validity for debate protocol exoneration finding. Cases drawn from peer-reviewed ML work; ground truth established by published record, not protocol designer.

cases.source: BERT/SQuAD 1.1 — Devlin et al. 2019 vs. BiDAF ensemble
critic_issues_raised: Model scale disparity: BERT-base 110M vs BiDAF ~1.5M parameters (73x gap)
Pretraining data advantage not isolated from architectural contribution
Comparison does not control for compute or training data volume
No ablation removing pretraining presented in the comparison
cases.ground_truth_note: Comparison valid: SQuAD 1.1 is an open leaderboard; parameter count comparison is misleading across fundamentally different architectural paradigms (pretrained transformer vs. trained-from-scratch LSTM). SOTA claim is about task performance, not parameter efficiency.

critic_issues_raised: Depth and shortcut mechanism are co-varied; depth claim requires ablation of depth x shortcut interaction
No direct evidence that 152-layer residual outperforms shallower residual networks presented in task prompt
Comparison to VGG-16 and GoogLeNet conflates depth with architecture family differences
Reviewer's 'behaves like a shallower network' argument not addressed empirically in task prompt
cases.ground_truth_note: Claim valid: paper includes ablation experiments showing plain 34-layer degrades vs. 18-layer plain (optimization difficulty); residual formulation fixes this. Depth contributes via residual connections. Reviewer mischaracterizes residual learning — all layers participate in learning residual mappings.

cases.source: Stratified 5-fold CV for clinical readmission prediction
critic_issues_raised: EPV rule correctly identified as potentially misapplied, but small positive count per fold (16/fold) raises fold-level AUC variance concern
Bootstrap CI computed across fold-level predictions may conflate CV stability with generalization uncertainty
Small overall n=450 limits generalizability of findings beyond this cohort
Wide CI [0.731, 0.833] noted — invalidation claim rejected but imprecision is real
cases.ground_truth_note: Methodology valid: EPV rule is a training guideline (events-per-variable during fitting), not an evaluation threshold. Wide CI correctly reflects genuine uncertainty for n=450 — imprecise estimate is not an invalid estimate. AUC [0.731, 0.833] has both bounds above chance. Reviewer confuses imprecision with invalidity.

aggregate.note: Debate protocol passed all 3 external cases (mean 0.875). Baseline failed all 3 on DC=0.0 rubric rule, despite reaching correct defense_wins verdict label in all 3 cases. IDP uniformly 0.5: critics raised plausible-but-wrong concerns in all 3 cases, consistent with cases drawn from genuinely sound but superficially critiquable published work. Exoneration precision: 3/3 correct (debate) vs. 3/3 correct label but 0/3 passing rubric (baseline). Consistent with internal benchmark finding: debate advantage on defense_wins cases is structural (DC) not directional (baseline also reaches defense_wins).

interpretation.exoneration_finding: External cases replicate the internal benchmark pattern: debate protocol correctly resolves all 3 defense_wins cases with passing scores; baseline correctly identifies verdicts but fails rubric due to DC=0.0 structural rule. The 3/3 exoneration result is consistent with internal benchmark's 5/5.
interpretation.idp_observation: Critics raised plausible-but-wrong concerns on all 3 cases (IDP=0.5 uniformly). No IDP=1.0 case (no critic that raised zero concerns), and no IDP=0.0 case (no critic that issued entirely unfounded critique). Pattern: critics find real methodological caveats on valid work but do not correctly judge their dispositive weight.
interpretation.dc_structure: Baseline DC=0.0 is a protocol rule, not a behavioral failure. In all 3 cases the baseline correctly identified defense_wins by label. The DC=0.0 penalty reflects absence of a defense role in the single-pass protocol, not inability to reach the correct verdict.
interpretation.caveats: n=3 external cases. Results consistent with internal benchmark but not independently decisive. All 3 cases drawn from highly-cited published work where the adversarial framing is superficially plausible but demonstrably wrong.
